questions.prompt: Three different customers in the same vertical have all asked for the same allowlist entry on their override layer. What's the right next move?
choices: Add the entry to the Universal Allow list
Promote the entry to the vertical overlay policy and remove the per-customer copies
Leave the per-customer entries in place, duplication is fine
Block the request because three asks isn't enough signal
questions.explanation: Three same-vertical asks is a vertical signal. Promote to the vertical overlay; remove the per-customer copies. Universal Allow is too wide, it would apply to other verticals too.

questions.prompt: In DNSFilter, a Universal Allow list entry overrides a Block list entry on the same policy.
questions.explanation: Allow lists always win, both Universal and per-policy. That's why scope discipline matters so much: a misplaced Universal Allow opens the door for every customer in the org.

questions.prompt: Which categories are 'always-block' (no customer conversation needed)? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Malware
Social Networking
Phishing
Cryptomining
Cloud Storage
Botnet / C2
questions.explanation: The threat-category set (Malware / Phishing / Cryptomining / Botnet) is non-negotiable for any business customer. Social Networking and Cloud Storage depend on the customer's business.

questions.prompt: If a customer needs a sanctioned VPN, the right pattern is to unblock the entire 'Anonymising proxies / VPNs' category for that customer.
questions.explanation: Unblocking the category opens the door to all VPN/anonymiser domains. The correct pattern is to leave the category blocked and add the specific sanctioned VPN domain to the policy's Allow list.

questions.prompt: DNSFilter's hosted block page is reliably displayed in the browser for blocked HTTPS queries.
questions.explanation: For HTTPS the browser sees a certificate mismatch and shows a TLS error instead of the block page. The hosted page is reliable for HTTP; HTTPS behaviour is browser-dependent and usually shows an error, not the block page.

questions.prompt: A customer's older playbook tells techs to enable a 'Bypass Password' on the DNSFilter block page so users can click through. What's the correct response in 2026?
choices: Enable it, it's still a supported feature
Enable it but cap the password length
Update the playbook. DNSFilter removed the Bypass Password in late 2024 over security concerns; the supported paths now are Allow-list entries and the block page's notice email
Build a custom external block page that re-implements the bypass
questions.explanation: DNSFilter removed the Bypass Password feature in late 2024. There is no direct replacement; access requests now go through the block page's embedded notice form, and exceptions are handled via Allow-list entries (per-policy or Universal).

questions.prompt: A customer's office has a residential-grade connection, the public IP changes every few weeks. How do you identify the Site to DNSFilter?
choices: Add a new Site each time the IP changes
Use a Dynamic DNS hostname; DNSFilter rechecks the DDNS record automatically
Skip the Site and rely on Roaming Clients only
Enter a /24 subnet that probably contains the changing IP
questions.explanation: DNSFilter supports Dynamic DNS hostnames as a Site identifier and rechecks the record automatically, so even residential-grade connections work cleanly.

questions.prompt: After a Site rollout, the Query Log shows zero queries from the Site's IP. Which of the following are plausible causes? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Browser DNS-over-HTTPS bypassing the network resolver
Apple iCloud Private Relay tunnelling DNS
User device manually configured to use 8.8.8.8
DNSFilter has automatically suspended the Site for low usage
questions.explanation: Zero queries means traffic isn't reaching DNSFilter. Common causes are encrypted-DNS paths (DoH, iCloud Private Relay) or users overriding DNS locally. DNSFilter doesn't auto-suspend Sites for low usage.

questions.prompt: Put the Intermediate-level site rollout steps in the correct order.
items: Create the Site (or push the Roaming Client) and assign a policy
Test on one device with example test domains
Cut the network DNS over to DNSFilter in a maintenance window
Watch the Query Log live for the first 30 minutes
questions.explanation: Always single-device test first; then the cutover; then watch for spikes. DNSFilter's own docs call out the single-device test as best practice before rolling out to the rest of the network.

questions.prompt: Emergency threat blocks during an active phishing campaign should still be phased through one device first.
questions.explanation: Emergency threat blocks reverse the normal cost equation: a missed legitimate site is a ticket, but a clicked phishing link is an incident. Push immediately to all affected sites.

questions.prompt: What does DNSFilter's Policy Audit Log actually record?
choices: Every DNS query and its verdict for the past year
Every change to filtering policies, who, what, when
Every authentication event for the dashboard
Every Roaming Client install and uninstall
questions.explanation: The Policy Audit Log is a record of policy *changes*, what was edited, by whom, and when. Per-query data lives in the DNS Query Log; auth events are separate.

questions.prompt: DNSFilter offers an 'audit-only' or 'monitor only' mode that you can apply to a category to log matches without blocking them.
questions.explanation: DNSFilter doesn't document a category-level audit-only mode. The phased-rollout discipline (test domains, single-device test, watch the Query Log live) is the documented substitute.
